title: A (slightly less) brief look into the restricted 3-body problem
speaker: Agustin Moreno
abstract: Despite the fact that the 3-body problem is an ancient conundrum that goes back to Newton, it is remarkably poorly understood, and is still a benchmark for modern developments. In this talk, I will give a (very) biased account of this classical problem, both from a modern theoretical perspective, i.e. outlining possible lines of attack coming from symplectic geometry, holomorphic curves and Floer theory; as well as comment on practical and numerical aspects, within the context of finding orbits for space mission design and ocean worlds exploration.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian and contact low-energy setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the circular restricted three-body problem as a Hamiltonian system with mass ratio and energy parameters, then recalls that low-energy regularized and compactified level sets are standard contact manifolds near each primary.

proof_stages.label: Poincare reduction by sections and open books
proof_stages.summary: The classical program is framed as finding global surfaces of section, passing to Poincare return maps, and recasting this in higher dimensions via open books adapted to the flow.

proof_stages.label: Spatial adapted open-book theorem
proof_stages.summary: For arbitrary mass ratio in the low-energy spatial problem, the regularized level set S^3 x S^2 admits an adapted open book with binding RP^3, page D^*S^2, and monodromy the square of the Dehn-Seidel twist, so spatial periodic orbits correspond to interior periodic points of the return map.

proof_stages.label: Vertical-angle fibration and collision interpolation
proof_stages.summary: The proof begins with the phase of q_3+ip_3, whose angular form is nonnegative on the vector field and vanishes only on the planar problem, and then resolves the failure at collisions by regularizing and interpolating with a collision-locus open book.

proof_stages.label: High-dimensional twist maps on Liouville domains
proof_stages.summary: Symplectic homology gives a high-dimensional Hamiltonian-twist-map analogue of Poincare-Birkhoff, but the natural twist condition is hard and not open in the three-body return map, motivating a broader study of Hamiltonian dynamics on Liouville pages.

proof_stages.label: Pseudoholomorphic shadow dynamics
proof_stages.summary: A Lefschetz fibration of D^*S^2 by annuli leads to a quotient S^3, and pseudoholomorphic annuli adapted to a contact form allow the original five-dimensional Reeb dynamics to be projected or averaged into a shadow dynamics whose surjective shadow map measures the complexity of the original problem.

proof_stages.label: Symmetric-orbit bifurcation invariants
proof_stages.summary: For practical orbit classification, symmetric periodic orbits are linearized at symmetry points to special symplectic matrices, and the associated GIT sequence and spectral bifurcation loci give computable topological obstructions to joining orbits by regular families.

proof_stages.label: SFT Euler characteristic and numerical validation
proof_stages.summary: Local Floer/SFT Euler characteristics remain invariant across bifurcation and can be used to test numerical orbit searches, with the Jupiter-Europa period-doubling computation illustrating a proposed database of symmetric orbits and bifurcations.

examples.name: Europa and Enceladus mission systems
examples.note: The Jupiter-Europa and Saturn-Enceladus systems motivate why classifying and optimizing restricted-three-body orbits has practical value for spacecraft design.

examples.name: Period-doubling bifurcation picture
examples.note: A standard bifurcation diagram from Abraham-Marsden illustrates how an elliptic eigenvalue crossing -1 produces a double-period orbit.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through the period-doubling picture by tracking eigenvalues crossing -1 and explaining how a double-cover orbit bifurcates into a doubled-period orbit

passes.reason: Period-doubling is a standard critical transition across the multiplier -1 bifurcation locus, used illustratively.
A period-doubling bifurcation is the critical degenerate crossing where an eigenvalue hits -1 and the double cover acquires eigenvalue 1.
A period-doubling bifurcation is the resonant critical crossing of multiplier -1, with the double iterate becoming degenerate at the boundary between regimes.
A period-doubling bifurcation is a critical resonance/degeneracy where a multiplier crosses the period-doubling locus, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
A period-doubling bifurcation is the critical multiplier-minus-one case, a degenerate boundary between stability regimes.
A period-doubling bifurcation occurs at the critical multiplier -1, where the double iterate is degenerate and the path crosses a stability boundary.
the period-doubling picture is a critical degenerate multiplier crossing on the boundary between spectral regimes, used illustratively
The period-doubling picture illustrates a critical bifurcation where an eigenvalue crosses the root-of-unity locus, so the orbit is degenerate and on a transition boundary.
A period-doubling bifurcation is a critical degenerate transition on the spectral boundary where a multiplier crosses -1.
A period-doubling bifurcation is the critical nonhyperbolic multiplier-crossing case where the double cover becomes degenerate, used illustratively.
period-doubling is a critical bifurcation locus where a multiplier crosses −1 and an iterate becomes degenerate
illustrates the critical multiplier-crossing boundary where a double-period orbit bifurcates

examples.name: Snitch configuration in the Jupiter-Europa problem
examples.note: A numerically produced Jupiter-Europa orbit family, nicknamed the snitch, demonstrates how the GIT quotient records a period-doubling bifurcation in computations.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker discusses a concrete numerically produced Snitch orbit in the Jupiter-Europa problem, including its period-doubling bifurcation and its path through regions of the GIT quotient
